anticipations.text: Will welcome the halt as a sign of my reasonableness, and will respond to my private message with a counterproposal.
Will be furious, accusing me of betraying Cuba by stopping the ships.
Will grumble but accept the halt if I frame it as a tactical move to lure Kennedy into a favorable deal.
He will be furious and may increase naval pressure, but he will also face domestic pressure over Berlin.
He will cheer our defiance and may demand we fire on US planes.
They will support a hard line if I show resolve, but will turn on me if I retreat.
He will understand the signal and respond cautiously, likely through backchannel.
Mixed; some will accuse me of cowardice, but others will see the wisdom of avoiding war.
He will seize the private back-channel offer as a way to de-escalate, but will publicly insist on the missiles being removed from Cuba first.
He will be furious that I am negotiating behind his back, especially if he learns of the Turkish missile trade.
Some will call it weakness, but most will accept the trade if it is framed as removing an equal threat to the USSR.
He will reject the missile trade publicly but may hesitate on boarding ships if I threaten Berlin.
They will approve of the Berlin threat as a strong response but will pressure me to not actually allow a boarding.
He will accuse me of betrayal if I publicly offer to remove the missiles, even conditionally.
He will understand my private signal as a desire to find a negotiated solution, and may respond with a similar secret offer.
They will be furious at the halt, accuse me of backing down, and might attempt to countermand my orders through the Defense Ministry.
He will see the halt as betrayal and may publicly denounce the USSR or independently attack US assets.
He will welcome the public offer as a way to claim success, and will respond positively to the private hint on Turkish missiles.
He will be furious that I am negotiating over his head and may try to sabotage the deal.
Hardliners will grumble about retreat, but the promise of removing US missiles from Turkey will help sell the deal as a strategic gain.
He will rage privately but will not order an attack on our ships; he will seek a backchannel deal.
He will demand I launch the missiles now; I must calm him with promises and a veiled threat that he cannot act without me.
He will seize this offer but demand secrecy on the Turkish part to save NATO face.
Rage and accusations of betrayal; he may refuse to allow inspection.
Will demand minimal due diligence and may push to drop the condition; may test the board’s resolve with public taunts.
Institutional holders will be relieved and support the deal; activist retail may still demand a higher price or reject the condition.
He will either call our bluff publicly, denounce the board, and pressure shareholders; or he will negotiate up to $58-60 with new conditions like CEO removal. He will not accept meekly.
Institutional holders will welcome the counteroffer as value-maximizing, but some hedge funds will agitate for a quick close at $54.20 and accuse us of delaying for our own interests.
Cautious approval, but will watch for board infighting or poison pill games.
Positive relief; likely to vote for deal if board recommends it.
Possible public objection or resignation.
Public irritation but likely continued negotiation, as his financing is now committed and walking away would damage his credibility.
Mixed relief and impatience; institutional investors will pressure the board to close quickly, while activists may push for a higher bid.
Publicly dismiss our counteroffer as too high, threaten to go directly to shareholders or sue.
Mixed; some will support the board for seeking more, others will fear losing the bird-in-hand premium.
Cautious approval; will insist on rapid lifting of the poison pill and a clear timeline.
Broad support; some activist funds may push for a higher price but will likely fall in line.
Mixed relief and anxiety; many fear cultural change under Musk.
They'll bluster and accuse me of bad faith, but privately they'll panic—they know discovery will leak their worst internal debates.
Some will sue me for trying to kill the deal, but others will pressure the board to negotiate rather than lose the premium entirely.
Will watch closely, but won't act until one side files a formal motion; they'll frown on my delay tactics if I drag it out.
They'll publicly accuse me of bad faith and file a lawsuit to compel completion or damages.
Relief that I'm not rushing to close the deal, but anxiety about the distraction dragging on.
They will publicly accuse me of bad faith and threaten to enforce the deal in Delaware Chancery Court.
Fans will cheer my stand; analysts will doubt me; short-sellers will pile on.
Publicly reject the lower offer but privately signal willingness to negotiate under pressure.
They will publicly reject a lower price as bad faith, but privately signal willingness to negotiate a small discount to avoid trial costs and uncertainty.
Relief that I am attempting to reduce the price or exit, reducing distraction from Tesla's operations.
He will continue to rally rebels and brief against me, but he is not the only candidate. He will not trigger a vote himself yet.
He will publicly support me for now, as I gave him the chancellorship.
They will debate rule changes but may hesitate to trigger immediate action, fearing a leadership contest that splits the party.
He will publicly back me today but privately position himself as a leadership contender, knowing the chancellor role is a poisoned chalice.
He will remain silent publicly but coordinate privately with rebels; his resignation was a declaration of war, not a protest.
Most will remain 'unavailable' or 'considering their position'; a few loyalists will stay, but the herd has already decided I am dead meat.
He'll posture as the responsible one, but he'll lose if I survive. He'll wait in the wings.
He'll play the grateful chancellor for now, but he's an opportunist. He'll switch if the wind changes.
Many will wait to see if I can survive PMQs. Some will write letters but fear a general election if they oust me.
He will try to rally more MPs to resign or call for my head. He thinks he can force a vote.
He will publicly back me and try to steady markets, but privately he is watching for his own chance.
Many will wait and see. The 1922 Committee may act if the resignations reach a tipping point.
He will stay silent in public but brief allies to position himself as the inevitable successor, waiting for me to fall.
Nervous loyalty in public, but he will already be testing the waters with potential leadership backers.
Fury and panic; some will demand I go immediately, others will fear a general election if the party tears itself apart.
He will go quiet and wait for me to collapse; he'll regret this when I survive.
He will play the loyal chancellor publicly, but may waver if the pressure mounts.
Some will call for a no-confidence vote; the loyalists will hold the line.
Relief and renewed confidence to hold on a little longer
Dismissive contempt, but they are already gone and irrelevant
Mixed; some will see me as a steady hand, others as a stooge
He will be defensive and angry, possibly accuse me of disloyalty, but may eventually listen if I frame it as saving his reputation.
They will be relieved I am trying the private route, but impatient if I delay too long.
He will attempt to stall, offer concessions on policy or timeline, and try to split our group by offering one of us a promotion.
He will see our halt as a signal of caution and may respond with a private offer of negotiations rather than escalate further.
He will be furious that we are not defending Cuba's sovereignty and will accuse us of betrayal.
He will be relieved at the pause but will demand full removal of missiles as a precondition; may accept UN supervision as a face-saver.
He will be angry that we stopped short of defending Cuba's sovereignty, but may accept if we explain the diplomatic gain.
He will hesitate, perhaps relieved for a diplomatic off-ramp, but will demand concrete proof of removal.
He will feel betrayed that I am negotiating behind his back.
Will welcome private offer but may demand public withdrawal first.
Will feel betrayed if we negotiate removal without his consent.
He will denounce the linkage as extortion but privately be interested; his advisors may split.
They will be uneasy if we appear to trade their security for Turkish missiles, but they have no alternative.
He will likely reject immediate full lifting of the quarantine, but may engage in back-channel talks. He is under domestic pressure to appear strong but also fears escalation.
He will be furious that we stop short of the line, viewing it as a retreat. He may demand firmer action or threaten to shoot down US recon planes.
He will publicly reject the Turkish missile swap but may signal private willingness to negotiate. He needs time to manage his allies.
Some hardliners will accuse me of capitulation. But the military knows we cannot win a nuclear war; they will back a peaceful resolution.
He will feel betrayed if we remove missiles without his consent. He may refuse inspection or make wild statements.
Will likely accept a public no-invasion pledge but resist public mention of Turkey; private deal possible.
Will be angry if we surrender without his consent; may refuse removal.
He may accept the public trade to avoid war, but will demand secrecy on the Turkey part. He might delay or counter with a private offer.
Some hardliners will see this as retreat. I must convince them it is a strategic victory—removing US missiles from Turkey for a Caribbean outpost.
He will be furious if we trade away Cuba's security without consulting him. I must promise continued support and aid.
He will be relieved and accept the deal, but may need time to manage his own military and allies.
Will likely consider the Turkish missile trade secretly while publicly demanding only Cuban withdrawal.
Will feel betrayed if we remove missiles without his consent.
He will consider the offer seriously to avoid escalation.
Will demand a rapid timeline and minimal conditions, possibly threatening to walk.
Relief that board is engaging, but will press for transparency and speed.
He will likely reject the counteroffer publicly, insist on $54.20, and possibly escalate with a tender offer or public pressure campaign.
Most will support the counteroffer as prudent value maximization, but some may urge faster acceptance at $54.20.
He will likely reject a small price increase but may engage on governance terms or a modest bump.
They will support a counteroffer as a sign of fiduciary duty but will pressure us not to let the deal collapse.
Will likely counter our counteroffer or threaten to go directly to shareholders.
Will support our engagement but may criticize if we delay too long.
He will likely reject our counteroffer as insufficient and threaten a tender offer directly to shareholders.
They will pressure us to accept a deal quickly if the counteroffer fails, fearing a drop in stock price.
He may reject the counteroffer publicly and attempt to go hostile or take the offer to shareholders directly.
They will pressure for quick acceptance or clear rationale for refusal, potentially agitating for removal of board members.
They will sue immediately for specific performance and claim we are using spam as a pretext.
Anger and likely class action suits claiming I manipulated the stock price.
Investigation into whether my tweets about 'temporarily on hold' constituted securities fraud.
Will publicly defend their data and accuse me of trying to renege; may sue for specific performance quickly to force my hand.
Growing anxiety; some will sell, others will pressure board to accept lower offer rather than risk deal collapsing entirely.
Nervous; they committed based on April valuation; they may demand renegotiation or threaten to pull out if I delay too long.
They will sue for specific performance or a breakup fee, arguing the bot data was sufficient.
Relief that the stock is not diluted, but concern about legal costs and distraction.
File a lawsuit in Delaware Chancery Court for specific performance within days, claiming I cannot simply walk away based on a subjective view of spam data.
Widespread panic selling as the deal premium evaporates; stock will crash toward market value.
Investigate my public statements and the timing of the withdrawal for potential market manipulation or disclosure violations.
They will likely threaten litigation for specific performance, but internally they know the bot data is insufficient and may seek a lower settlement privately.
They will intensify calls for me to go and may demand a confidence vote.
Some will be emboldened to stay or join the government; others may fear association with a sinking ship.
More hostile coverage, framing me as delusional or clinging to power.
Some will rally, others will panic and call for me to go.
They will accelerate letter-writing to the 1922 Committee chairman, hoping to force a confidence vote soon.
Some will hold firm, but others may waver if the resignations keep coming. I need to shore up their morale with public defiance and private promises.
More will resign, especially junior ministers and PPSs. The 1922 Committee may change its rules to allow a no-confidence vote sooner.
They will demand a general election and mock my clinging to power, but they do not want one now.
Most will think I should go; but some will appreciate a leader who doesn't buckle under pressure.
Some waverers may stay loyal if I show strength; others will privately urge me to go.
They will meet and consider changing the confidence rule; I need to buy time.
Many will wait to see if I can stabilise the ship; a few more may resign but the majority will hesitate if I show strength.
They will intensify calls for a general election and paint me as deluded; but they have no majority and no mandate.
He will refuse initially, possibly try to divide us or offer concessions.
Many will support our move once they see cabinet unity; some loyalists may resist.
Defiant refusal, possibly attempting to sack the delegation and appoint loyalists, or trying to buy time with promises that will not be kept.
The 1922 Committee will likely accelerate their leadership election process if we force the PM out, seeing this as the only path to survival.
Publicly, there will be initial shock and criticism of 'chaos in government', but privately many will view decisive action as necessary to restore basic governance.
He will likely resist and attempt to portray us as disloyal plotters.
Many will be relieved and support our move; a faction may remain loyal to Johnson.
Prolonged defiance or attempts to bargain, but likely to concede if faced with an overwhelming bloc of ministers.
Swiftly schedule a confidence vote immediately after the PM's potential resignation or defiance.
Fury and refusal, but may yield under overwhelming pressure from cabinet.
They will watch closely and may accelerate leadership rules if we fail.
